sanskrit_devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः ।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि स योगी मयि वर्तते ॥ ३१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sarva-bhūta-sthitaṁ yo māṁ bhajaty ekatvam āsthitaḥ |
sarvathā vartamāno 'pi sa yogī mayi vartate || 31 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Such a yogī, who engages in the worshipful service of the Supersoul, knowing that I and the Supersoul are one, remains always in Me in all circumstances.
The yogi who is established in union with Me, and worships Me as the Supreme Soul residing in all beings, dwells only in Me, though engaged in all kinds of activities.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2042 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A yogī who is practicing meditation on the Supersoul sees within himself the plenary portion of Kṛṣṇa as Viṣṇu – with four hands, holding conchshell, wheel, club and lotus flower. The yogī should know that Viṣṇu is not different from Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa in this form of Supersoul is situated in everyone’s heart.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

positions.rendering: knowing that I and the Supersoul are one
fixed in the realisation reiterated from 6.30 — sees me everywhere, sees all in me
fixed in the state of Yoga in oneness because he has the same form of uncontracted knowledge as Myself, worshipping by renouncing the differences of prakṛti

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Sankara reads ekatvam as the non-dual oneness of the realised self with Brahman; Ramanuja reads it as the qualified oneness of the jīva with Īśvara via shared uncontracted knowledge; Prab. flattens to identity-with-Supersoul. The operational claim — that the realised yogi's state is preserved across all conduct — is shared across traditions.

literal_meaning: The yogi who, established in oneness, worships me dwelling in all beings — that yogi, however he may move, abides in me.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.31 names the post-attainment state. The yogi who has reached ekatvam — the oneness named in 6.30 — worships not by ritual alone but by recognising the same one (māṁ) as already sthita (situated) in all beings. Sankara's reading: this verse states the result of the realization of 6.30, namely Liberation — such a one exists in Me in whatever way he conducts himself. Ramanuja's reading: the yogin fixed in oneness, worshipping by renouncing the differences of prakṛti, even when emerging from samādhi and engaged in ordinary action, abides in Me. Both traditions preserve: post-realisation, no condition of life takes the yogi out of the established state. The verse is metaphysically loaded; the engineering analog is single-life and operational.
